analogy: Think of "Sound" through the chapter's own opening images: LIGHT LIGHT The world is largely known through the senses. The sense of sight is one of the most important senses. Through it we see mountains, rivers, trees, plants, chairs, people and so many other things around us. We also see clouds, rainbows and birds... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LIGHT LIGHT, 13.1 What makes Things, 13.2 Laws of Reflection, SCIENCE 166. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Sound is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that lIGHT LIGHT The world is largely known through the senses.

The text explains that the sense of sight is one of the most important senses.

The text explains that through it we see mountains, rivers, trees, plants, chairs, people and so many other things around us.

The text explains that we also see clouds, rainbows and birds flying in the sky.

The text explains that you are able to see the words and sentences printed on this page.

The text explains that 13.1 What makes Things Visible Have you ever thought how we see various objects?

The text explains that it means that eyes alone cannot see any object.

The text explains that it is only when light from an object enters our eyes that we see the object.

The text explains that the light may have been emitted by the object, or may have been reflected by it.

The text explains that you learnt in Class VII that a polished or a shiny surface can act as a mirror.

The text explains that a mirror changes the direction of light that falls on it.

The text explains that can you tell in which direction the light falling on a surface will be reflected?

The text explains that 13.2 Laws of Reflection Activity 13.1 Fix a white sheet of paper on a drawing board or a table.

The text explains that take a comb and close all its openings except one in the middle.

The text explains that you can use a strip of black paper for this purpose.

The text explains that hold the comb perpendicular to the sheet of paper.

The text explains that after striking the mirror, the ray of light is reflected in another direction.

The text explains that what do you infer? with the Sun as the source of light instead of a torch.

The text explains that you, too, can use the Sun as the source of light.

The text explains that boojho remembered that in Class VII, he had studied some features of the image of an object formed by a plane mirror.

The text explains that paheli asked him to recall those features: (i) Was the image erect or upside down?

The text explains that let us understand a little more about the formation of an image by a plane mirror in the following way: Activity 13.3 A source of light O is placed in front of a plane mirror PQ.

The text explains that can you find out the direction of the reflected rays?

The text explains that you may recall that in an image formed by a mirror the left of the object appears on the right and the right appears on the left.

The text explains that you will find that these rays are reflected in different directions.

The text explains that can you explain how reflection from the two mirrors enables you to see objects which are not visible directly?

The text explains that 13.9 : Mirror at the hair dresser shop I have a question.

The text explains that set them at right angles to each other with their edges touching (Fig.

The text explains that 13.10 : Images in plane mirror at right angle to each other Fig.

The text explains that 13.11 : Image in plane mirror parallel to each other mirrors (a) (b) (c) Can you now explain how you can see the back of your head at the hair dresser’s shop?

The text explains that an interesting feature of a kaleidoscope is that you will never see the same pattern again.

The text explains that it is, therefore, important to understand its structure and working.

The text explains that it is tough so that it can protect the interior of the eye from accidents.

The text explains that put this arrangement near a window in such a way that direct sunlight falls on the mirror.

The text explains that in which case do you need to allow more light in the eye, when the light is dim or bright?

The text explains that behind the cornea, we find a dark muscular structure called iris.

The text explains that there are two kinds of cells– (i) cones, which are sensitive to bright light and (ii) rods, which are sensitive to dim light.

The text explains that its existence can be demonstrated as follows: Activity 13.9 Make a round mark and a cross on a sheet of paper with the spot to the right of the cross (Fig.

The text explains that sCIENCE 174 lamp or a laser torch can injure the retina. l Do not look at the Sun or a powerful light directly. l Never rub your eyes.

The text explains that however, additional resources can enable them to develop their capabilities further.

The text explains that the most popular resource for visually challenged persons is Braille.

The text explains that louis Braille Louis Braille, himself a visually challenged person, developed a system for visually challenged persons and published it in 1821.

The text explains that many Indian languages can be read using the Braille system.

The text explains that braille system has 63 dot patterns or characters.

The text explains that 13.18 : Example of dot patterns used in Braille System 2024-25

The text explains that keller Visually impaired people learn the Braille system by beginning with letters, then special characters and letter combinations.

The text explains that (ii) Incident ray, reflected ray and the normal drawn at the point of incidence to the reflecting surface, lie in the same plane. ‹ Image formed in a plane mirror undergoes lateral inversion. ‹ Two mirrors inclined to each other give multiple images. ‹ Beautiful patterns are formed in a kaleidoscope because of multiple reflections. ‹ Sunlight, called white light, consists of seven colours. ‹ Splitting of light into its constituent colours is known as dispersion. ‹ Parts of the eye are cornea, iris, pupil, lens, retina and optic nerve. ‹ A normal eye can see nearby and distant objects clearly. ‹ Visually impaired persons can read and write using the Braille system. ‹ Visually impaired persons develop their other senses more sharply to improve their interac- tion with their environment.

The text explains that (b) If you touch your ____________ ear with right hand in front of a plane mirror it will be seen in the mirror that your right ear is touched with ____________.

The text explains that choose the correct option in Questions 7 – 8 7.

The text explains that can you explain the basis of the teacher’s advise?

The text explains that make friends with some visually impaired students.

The text explains that also find out how they are able to recognise objects, hurdles and currency notes.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 13: Sound

prelims_traps: Do not treat LIGHT LIGHT and 13.1 What makes Things as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Sound; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Sound through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LIGHT LIGHT and 13.1 What makes Things to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
